claims.claim_text: The opening healthcare incident includes a specific March 2020 timeline, polling interval, monthly cost, batch size, and processing delay.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: In March 2020, a healthcare data platform needed to process incoming patient lab results in near real-time. Each result arrived as a PDF uploaded to a Cloud Storage bucket by external lab partners. The original architecture used a VM that polled the bucket every 30 seconds, downloaded new files, parsed the PDFs, extracted structured data, and published it to downstream systems. This polling approach had three problems. First, the 30-second polling interval meant results could be delayed by up to 30 seconds---unacceptable for critical lab values. Second, the VM ran 24/7 even though uploads only happened during business hours, costing $450 per month in idle compute. Third, when a lab partner accidentally uploaded 15,000 files in a single batch (a backlog dump), the single VM took 6 hours to process them all, creating a dangerous delay in critical result delivery.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A polling VM that checks Cloud Storage on a fixed interval can add delivery lag, waste idle compute, and struggle when a large backlog arrives at once.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed war story with fake-precise operational and cost details.

claims.claim_text: The follow-up paragraph claims a specific migration outcome with exact scaling, runtime, monthly cost, and build-time figures.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2
claims.anchor_text: The team replaced the polling VM with a Cloud Function triggered by Cloud Storage events. Each upload immediately triggered the function, eliminating the polling delay. Cloud Functions automatically scaled to process 15,000 files concurrently, completing the batch in under 4 minutes instead of 6 hours. The monthly compute cost dropped from $450 to $12 because the functions only ran when files were uploaded. The total engineering time to build and deploy the solution was two days.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Replacing a polling VM with an event-driven function can reduce idle cost and improve burst handling because compute runs only when uploads arrive and the platform can scale out automatically.
claims.rationale: This continues the same unsourced incident with fabricated precision.

claims.claim_text: Gen 2 functions are Cloud Run services, support up to 1000 concurrent requests per instance, and support traffic splitting, unlike Gen 1.
claims.span_hint: section: Gen 1 vs Gen 2 table; section: Why Gen 2 matters
claims.rationale: The official comparison page directly covers the Cloud Run resource model, concurrency, and traffic-splitting differences.

claims.claim_text: The table says Gen 2 max timeout is 3600 seconds without distinguishing HTTP from event-driven functions.
claims.span_hint: section: Gen 1 vs Gen 2 table, Max timeout row
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Max timeout** | 540 seconds (9 minutes) | Depends on function type; HTTP functions can run longer than event-driven functions |
claims.rationale: Google's quotas distinguish Gen 2 HTTP timeout limits from Gen 2 event-driven timeout limits, so the unqualified row is inaccurate.

claims.claim_text: Gen 1 max memory is 8 GiB and Gen 2 max memory is 32 GiB.
claims.span_hint: section: Gen 1 vs Gen 2 table, Max memory row
claims.rationale: The Cloud Run functions quotas page lists 8 GiB for 1st gen and 32 GiB for 2nd gen.

claims.claim_text: The table says Gen 2 event triggers include Eventarc with 100+ event sources.
claims.span_hint: section: Gen 1 vs Gen 2 table, Event triggers row
claims.anchor_text: | **Event triggers** | Cloud Storage, Pub/Sub, HTTP, Firestore | All of Gen 1 + Eventarc (100+ event sources) |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Event triggers** | Cloud Storage, Pub/Sub, HTTP, Firestore | Gen 1 triggers plus Eventarc-supported direct events and audit-log-based routing |
claims.rationale: The exact numeric source count is unstable and not directly stated on one authoritative page for this phrasing.

claims.claim_text: Cloud Storage direct events for Gen 2/Eventarc include finalized, deleted, archived, and metadataUpdated object events.
claims.span_hint: section: Cloud Storage Triggers, Event Types table
claims.rationale: The Eventarc event types page explicitly lists these Cloud Storage event types.

claims.claim_text: The Eventarc overview sentence says it connects 100+ GCP services to Cloud Functions, Cloud Run, GKE, and Workflows.
claims.span_hint: section: Eventarc: The Event Router, opening sentence
claims.anchor_text: Eventarc is the unified event routing layer for GCP. It connects event sources (Cloud Storage, Pub/Sub, Cloud Audit Logs, and 100+ GCP services) to event targets (Cloud Functions, Cloud Run, GKE, Workflows).
claims.suggested_rewrite: Eventarc is GCP's event routing layer. It connects sources such as Cloud Storage, Pub/Sub, and Cloud Audit Logs to targets such as Cloud Functions, Cloud Run, GKE, and Workflows.
claims.rationale: The number is unnecessarily precise here and not needed for the teaching point.

claims.claim_text: A Pub/Sub trigger calls the function whenever a message is published to the specified topic.
claims.span_hint: section: Pub/Sub Integration, opening paragraph and deploy example context
claims.rationale: The official Pub/Sub trigger docs describe this exact trigger behavior.

claims.claim_text: The Cloud Storage retry row says the default is retries for 7 days.
claims.span_hint: section: Error Handling and Retries, Retry Behavior table, Cloud Storage row
claims.anchor_text: | **Cloud Storage** | Retries for 7 days | Yes (can disable) |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Cloud Storage** | Retry behavior depends on how the trigger and function were created | Yes |
claims.rationale: The module collapses multiple retry models together; Cloud Functions v2 API defaults and Eventarc transport defaults are not equivalent.

claims.claim_text: The Pub/Sub retry row says the default is retries until the ack deadline.
claims.span_hint: section: Error Handling and Retries, Retry Behavior table, Pub/Sub row
claims.anchor_text: | **Pub/Sub** | Retries until ack deadline | Yes (ack deadline, retry policy) |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Pub/Sub** | Redelivery behavior depends on the subscription and function retry settings | Yes |
claims.rationale: This wording is too absolute and hides the distinction between Pub/Sub transport policy and function-level retry configuration.

claims.claim_text: Automatic retrying is not available for HTTP functions.
claims.span_hint: section: Error Handling and Retries, Retry Behavior table, HTTP row
claims.rationale: The retry documentation states this explicitly.

claims.claim_text: Event-driven functions are at-least-once and should be idempotent because duplicate calls can occur.
claims.span_hint: section: Idempotency: The Golden Rule
claims.rationale: The retry guide covers at-least-once execution and recommends idempotent handling for duplicates.

claims.claim_text: Deploying a Gen 2 function creates a Cloud Run service and the function appears in the Cloud Run console.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 1
claims.rationale: The deployment guide says the deployment creates the Cloud Run-managed artifact and the function appears on the Cloud Run overview page.

claims.claim_text: The module claims Cloud Functions can be triggered by over 120 GCP event types and then lists example services.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 2
claims.anchor_text: 2. **Cloud Functions can be triggered by over 120 GCP event types** through Eventarc. This includes events from services you might not expect: BigQuery job completions, Cloud SQL instance restarts, IAM policy changes, and even GKE cluster events. Any GCP service that writes to Cloud Audit Logs can trigger a function.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 2. **Cloud Functions can be triggered by many Google Cloud event types** through Eventarc. Direct events and audit-log-based triggers let you react to activity across a wide range of GCP services.
claims.rationale: The exact count is not the important lesson and is not cleanly anchored by one authoritative page in this wording.

claims.claim_text: The cold-start bullet gives exact runtime-specific latency ranges and a monthly min-instances cost estimate.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 3
claims.anchor_text: 3. **The cold start time for a Python Cloud Function is typically 200-800 milliseconds**. For Node.js, it is 100-500ms. For Java, it can be 3-10 seconds due to JVM startup. Setting `--min-instances=1` on Gen 2 functions eliminates cold starts by keeping at least one instance warm at all times (at a cost of ~$5-10/month for a small function).
claims.suggested_rewrite: 3. **Cold start latency varies by runtime, dependency set, and configuration**. Using minimum instances can reduce cold-start latency for latency-sensitive functions, but it adds ongoing cost because warm instances stay allocated.
claims.rationale: These timing and cost figures are workload- and region-dependent and are not stable enough to leave as fixed facts.

claims.claim_text: The module says Eventarc filtering uses CEL expressions for cases like CSV extensions or specific zones.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 4
claims.anchor_text: 4. **Eventarc can filter events using CEL (Common Expression Language) expressions**. This means you can create triggers that only fire for specific conditions---for example, only when a file with a `.csv` extension is uploaded, or only when a VM in a specific zone is created. This reduces unnecessary function invocations and saves money.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 4. **Eventarc supports event filtering**. You can narrow which events reach a target so the function runs only for the resources and changes you care about.
claims.rationale: This overstates CEL usage for the module's Eventarc Standard trigger context and mixes in unsupported filtering details.

claims.claim_text: The timeout row says Gen 2 supports up to 3600s without distinguishing trigger type.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, timeout row
claims.anchor_text: | Setting timeout too low | Default of 60s seems enough | Set timeout based on worst-case processing time; Gen 2 supports up to 3600s |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Setting timeout too low | Short defaults can look safe in testing | Set timeout from worst-case processing time and check the trigger-specific limits |
claims.rationale: The unqualified 3600s statement is inaccurate because event-driven functions have different limits than HTTP functions.

claims.claim_text: The concurrency row says the Gen 2 default is 1, the same as Gen 1.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, concurrency row
claims.anchor_text: | Not using concurrency on Gen 2 | Default is 1 (same as Gen 1) | Set `--concurrency=80` for I/O-bound functions to reduce instances and cost |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Not using concurrency on Gen 2 | Default settings are not always a good fit | Tune concurrency for the workload, especially for I/O-bound functions |
claims.rationale: Cloud Run's default concurrency is not 1, so the row's premise is wrong even though deliberate tuning is good advice.

claims.claim_text: If you do not specify a service account, Cloud Run functions uses the Compute Engine default service account, which may have the Editor role on the project.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, dedicated service account row
claims.rationale: The Cloud Run functions IAM page documents the default runtime service account behavior and the Editor-role caveat.

further_reading.title: Compare Cloud Run Functions
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the primary reference for Gen 1 versus Gen 2 architecture, concurrency, and traffic-management differences.

further_reading.title: Trigger Functions from Cloud Storage Using Eventarc
further_reading.why_relevant: It shows the canonical Cloud Storage to Eventarc to function flow used throughout the module.

further_reading.title: Configure Event-Driven Function Retries
further_reading.why_relevant: It covers retry defaults, at-least-once delivery, idempotency, and transport-level retry behavior.

notes: Code blocks, Mermaid diagrams, quiz items, and hands-on exercise steps were intentionally excluded. The retry section currently conflates Cloud Functions v2 retry defaults with underlying Eventarc and Pub/Sub transport behavior.
